notes.note: Heading: Cicero to Servius [Sulpicius Rufus], greetings. The recipient is Servius Sulpicius Rufus, the great jurist, then proconsul of Achaea 46--45 BC; not his son of the same name. S. = salutem dicit.

notes.anchor: on the matter of L. Mescinius, most welcome of all
notes.note: Lucius Mescinius Rufus, apparently an eques and man of literary interests closely connected to Cicero; he had business pending in Achaea (Sulpicius's province) and Cicero had evidently written an earlier letter of recommendation on his behalf.

notes.anchor: no further claim shall be made under that head
notes.note: The formula amplius eo nomine non peti (no further claim shall be made under that head) was a standard suretyship release in Roman inheritance disputes, confirming that a creditor or litigant would pursue no further action once terms were met; Cicero offers his own surety (fides) to guarantee it.

notes.anchor: Oppia, who was Mindius's wife, has carried off
notes.note: Oppia, widow of a Mindius, is accused of misappropriating property that forms part of an estate in which Mescinius has an interest; bringing her to Rome (in iudicium, to trial) is the legal remedy Cicero urges Sulpicius to facilitate.

notes.anchor: those studies of ours, in which we formerly took delight and by which we now actually live
notes.note: A recurring Ciceronian motif (especially post-49 BC): after being shut out of political life by Caesar's dominance, Cicero found refuge in philosophy and letters; the phrase nunc etiam vivimus (we now actually live by them) reflects his self-presentation in the period of the Tusculanae and De finibus.
